generate.staleBanner: ⚠️ Settings changed — regenerate to update your bake plan
generate.emptyBakePlan: Complete your dough setup to generate your bake plan
generate.confirmOverwrite: This will overwrite your saved bake plan. Continue?

pizzaParty.comingSoon: Topping selector and shopping list coming soon. Your dough plan is ready in the Bake Plan tab.

pizza.label: Pizza
pizza.desc: Neapolitan, New York, Pizza Romana, Roman Teglia, Detroit & Sourdough

bread.label: Bread
bread.desc: Pain au levain, Pain de campagne, Baguette & more

quantity.largeBatchNote: Large batch — consider splitting into two sessions

results.computeError: Could not compute recipe — please check your style selection and schedule times.
results.largeBatchDesc: Large dough mass retains heat longer — fermentation may be faster than calculated. Fine-tune if needed.

scheduler.bakeTimeLabelPizza: When does it go in the oven?
scheduler.bakeTimeLabelBread: When does it go in the oven?
scheduler.bakeTimeSub: Baker Hub builds your schedule backwards from here.
scheduler.startBeforeBake: Start time must be before bake time.
scheduler.startMovedNote: Start moved to {time} to avoid your unavailability block.

scenario.plentyPreferredSub: Starting a few hours later is still great — just a shorter cold rest.
scenario.plentySub: Anywhere from {start} to {end} gives good results.
scenario.tight: Tight window — start as soon as possible.
scenario.tooShort: Very little time — start now for the best you can get.

blockers.heading: Any time constraints?
blockers.sub: Optional — add your unavailable windows and we'll plan around them.
blockers.noOvernights: No overnight periods in this schedule.

scheduler.adjustDoughVerb: drag the green diamond — curve should be near its peak at Bake
scheduler.adjustPrefVerb: drag the gold diamond — curve should be near its peak at Start Dough

hydration.noteStiff: Dough will be quite stiff — a little more water may help with stretching.
hydration.noteBelowClassic: Below the {name} classic range. Dough will be firmer and a bit denser.
hydration.noteClassic: Authentic {name} range. Great handling and traditional texture.
hydration.noteExtended: More open crumb and airiness. Requires confident shaping technique.
hydration.noteExtreme: Extreme hydration. Expect very sticky dough — wet hands, bench scraper essential.
hydration.noteHigh: High hydration territory. Excellent open crumb but challenging to handle.

oil.traditionalHighTemp: Traditional — no oil. Works beautifully at high-temp (450°C+).
oil.traditionalHome: Classic {style} uses no oil. Keeps the dough extensible and light.
oil.highTempNote: For best results in a high-temp oven, keeping oil at 0% works beautifully — oil can cause burning at very high temps.
oil.home1: Helps browning and tenderness at home oven temps. Classic choice for home baking.
oil.home2: Pan pizza range — creates a crispy, almost-fried base. Right at home in Detroit and pan styles.
oil.high: Entering enriched dough territory — high oil softens gluten development. An osmotolerant yeast (SAF Gold) helps.

sugar.traditionalHighTemp: Traditional — no sugar. Works beautifully at high-temp.
sugar.traditionalHome: Classic. Add 0.5% to help caramelisation in a home oven below 280°C.
sugar.subtle: Subtle colour boost. Good for home oven baking below 280°C.
sugar.noticeable: Noticeable sweetness and good browning. Works well for enriched styles.
sugar.osmotic: Above 2%, sugar adds osmotic stress on yeast — fermentation slows. Add extra 10% yeast.
sugar.high: High sugar territory (brioche level) — standard yeast may struggle. SAF Gold recommended.

preferment.heading: Preferment
preferment.subtitle: Optional head start for flavour and structure.

simple.instant: Fine powder sachet. Most reliable, longest shelf life.
simple.active_dry: Brown granules. Widely available. Dissolve in warm water first.
simple.fresh: Soft beige block. Slightly richer flavour. Use within 2 weeks.
simple.sourdough: Wild fermentation. Deeper flavour, better digestibility. Needs an active starter.

custom.active_dry: Brown granules · ×1.33 vs IDY · proof in 38°C water
custom.fresh: Soft block · ×3.0 vs IDY · keep refrigerated
custom.sourdough: Wild yeast · replaces formula · needs active starter at peak

avpn.limitDesc: 280g max per ball — the Associazione Verace Pizza Napoletana standard for authentic Neapolitan pizza.
avpn.body: The AVPN defines the official standard for authentic Neapolitan pizza. Maximum ball weight: 280g. Diameter: 22–35cm. This ensures the proper ratio of crust to topping and the characteristic light, airy cornicione.

climate.cityNotFound: City not found. Try a different spelling or nearby city.
climate.warmNudge: In a warm climate, afternoon temps can push above 28°C. If your kitchen heats up during the day, consider entering your expected peak temperature instead.
climate.fridgeWarning: Fridge at {temp}°C is warmer than the standard 4°C — yeast will be more active during cold retard.
climate.heading: Your kitchen
climate.subtitle: Your climate decides how fast the dough ferments.

timeline.autoAdjusted: Schedule auto-adjusted — a room temperature window was too long for your kitchen temperature. A cold retard phase was added automatically to prevent over-fermentation.

steps.restRT: Remove from fridge — rest at room temperature

prefSteps.tipPoolishFridge: Mix equal weight flour and water (100% hydration) with a pinch of yeast. Stir until combined. Cover tightly and place in the fridge.
prefSteps.tipPoolishRT: Mix equal weight flour and water (100% hydration) with a pinch of yeast. Stir until no dry flour remains. Leave at room temperature.
prefSteps.tipBiga: Combine flour, water and a tiny amount of yeast (0.1–0.2%). Mix roughly — biga is intentionally shaggy, not smooth. Cover and refrigerate.
prefSteps.tipRemoveBiga: Take your biga out of the fridge — no warmup needed, biga goes straight into the mix cold.
prefSteps.tipRemovePoolishShort: Take your poolish out of the fridge now — time is short before mixing. Watch for peak rather than the clock.
prefSteps.tipFeedHot: Feed equal weights starter + flour + water. At {temp} your starter peaks in 3–5h — watch for dome + bubbles.
prefSteps.tipFeedWarm: Feed equal weights starter + flour + water. Peaks in 4–6h at {temp}.
prefSteps.tipFeedRT: Feed equal weights starter + flour + water. Peaks in 6–10h at room temperature.

mixTips.sourdough: Combine flour and water, then add your starter. Add salt with the remaining water. Mix until fully absorbed.
mixTips.hand: Flour + 90% water first — cover and rest 20 min (autolyse). Then yeast, salt, remaining water, and knead 8–12 min.
mixTips.spiral: Flour + water + yeast at Speed 1. Add salt, then Speed 2 until pumpkin shape forms and the windowpane test passes.
mixTips.noKnead: Combine all ingredients and mix until no dry flour remains — time and stretch & folds do the rest.
mixTips.stand: Flour + water at Speed 1. Add yeast, then salt, then remaining water. Speed 2 until dough clears the bowl and passes the windowpane test.

bulkTips.long: Cover tightly and place in a warm spot away from drafts. Perform 4 sets of stretch & folds in the first 2 hours.
bulkTips.medium: Cover tightly. Perform 2–3 sets of stretch & folds every 20–30 minutes.
bulkTips.short: Cover tightly. Perform one set of stretch & folds after 15 minutes, then cover and rest.
bulkTips.veryShort: Cover tightly and rest — dough goes straight to the fridge after this short window.

coldTips.bulk: Whole dough mass goes into the fridge. Cold bulk fermentation slows yeast activity and develops flavour. No action needed.
coldTips.balls: Individual dough balls rest in the fridge. This firms them up and makes final proofing more controlled.
coldTips.single: Dough rests in the fridge. Cold fermentation builds flavour slowly — no action needed, time works for you.

timeline.divideTip: Weigh and divide dough into {n} ball{plural}. Pinch the bottom tight for surface tension.
timeline.divideTipCold: Cold dough is easier to ball — work quickly before it warms up.
timeline.divideTipWarm: ⚠️ Your kitchen is warm — get balls back in the fridge within 20 minutes.

finalProofTips.withCold: Remove balls from fridge and rest until slightly soft, then proof covered at room temperature. Start preheating your oven during the final proof.
finalProofTips.withoutCold: Shape dough balls and proof covered at room temperature. Start preheating your oven during the final proof.

preheatTips.long: Start now — while dough finishes its final proof. Give it the full {min} min to fully saturate your baking surface.
preheatTips.short: Start now — while dough finishes its final proof. Set oven to maximum temperature for {min} minutes.

timeline.eatTip: Your dough is perfectly fermented. Score, load, and bake. Buon appetito!
timeline.restRtTip: Take dough balls out of the fridge and leave covered at room temperature. Cold dough is too stiff to stretch — wait until slightly soft.
timeline.finalProofCovered: Balls are already shaped — cover and leave at room temperature until the poke test.
timeline.finalProofShape: Shape your balls if not done. Cover and leave at room temperature until the poke test.

recipeOutput.largeBatchFinalDough: Your final dough is ~{grams}g — more than your {mixer} can handle at once. Split into {n} batches.
recipeOutput.largeBatchTotal: Your total dough is {grams}g — more than your {mixer} can handle at once. Split into {n} batches.
recipeOutput.precisionScaleBody: Your yeast amount is {amount} — too small for a standard kitchen scale. Use a precision scale accurate to 0.1g, or use the dilution method.
recipeOutput.dilutionTitle: Dilution required — amount too small to weigh directly
recipeOutput.poolishBody: Long room-temperature fermentation is hard to control. A poolish gives you better flavour and more predictable timing. Mix 50% of your flour with an equal weight of water and a pinch of yeast 8–16h before baking.
recipeOutput.notRecommendedTitle: This fermentation schedule is not recommended
recipeOutput.notRecommendedBody: The combination of time and temperature will likely cause over-fermentation. Add a cold retard phase or reduce your start time.
recipeOutput.flourTooltipBread: Strong bread flour works best — T65 or W200+. Plain flour works for short ferments.
recipeOutput.flourTooltipPizza: Italian 00 or T45 forte gives the best results. Using plain flour or T55? Switch to Custom mode to adapt your recipe.

starterReady.floatTest: Float test: drop a piece in water — floats ✓

recipeOutput.waterKeeperNote: Keeps dough at target temperature despite mixer friction
recipeOutput.precisionScaleMin: Precision scale recommended — this is a very small amount of yeast.
recipeOutput.priorityFlavourNote: 🟢 Flavour-first yeast — you have plenty of time
recipeOutput.prioritySpeedNote: ⚡ Speed-adjusted yeast — your window is tight
recipeOutput.starterFeedNote: Feed your starter 4–12h before mixing. Use it at peak activity — doubled in size, domed on top.
recipeOutput.discardQuestion: What to do with the discard?
recipeOutput.discardNote1: Discard the rest before feeding — or use it for pancakes, crackers or flatbread 🥞
recipeOutput.discardNote2: Never throw all your starter away — always keep at least 20–30g.

schedulePicker.guardPast: This bake time has passed — try picking a later date.
schedulePicker.guardShort: Working with a short window — same-day dough can still be wonderful.
schedulePicker.blockerNote: Start Dough falls in one of your busy windows — that's fine if it works for you.
schedulePicker.bulkNote: Your dough needs ~{dur} to rise — be free by {time} to put it in the fridge, or start a bit earlier.
schedulePicker.fallbackBtn1: Start just before or after my busy time
schedulePicker.fallbackBtn2: Mix during my busy time — I'll make it work
schedulePicker.fallbackBtn2Sub: Still within the ideal window — dough will be excellent.
schedulePicker.gotIt: Got it, I'll make it work

simpleStatus.earlyOk: 🟡 A little early — should still turn out well
simpleStatus.lateOk: 🟡 Slightly late — dough will be fine

schedulePicker.doughEarlyOk: 🟡 Peaks just before bake — should be fine
schedulePicker.doughLateOk: 🟡 Still rising at bake — should be fine
schedulePicker.doughPeaksBefore: 🔴 Dough peaks before bake — over-fermentation risk
schedulePicker.prefEarlyOk: 🟡 Just past peak — use it, it'll be fine
schedulePicker.prefLateOk: 🟡 Still developing — use it, it'll be fine
schedulePicker.prefInFridgeReady: 🧊 In fridge → ready at Start Dough

flourNote.short: Pizza or bread flour works well for this plan.
flourNote.medium: Pizza flour gives the best results — Italian 00 is ideal. French T45 forte works too 🌾
flourNote.long: Strong pizza flour recommended — Italian 00 W270+. French alternative: T45 forte W260+ 🌾
flourNote.adapt: Using plain flour or T55? Tap to adapt →
flourNote.breadShort: Bread flour works well for this plan.
flourNote.breadMedium: Strong bread flour recommended — T65 or bread flour W200+ 🌾
flourNote.breadLong: High-protein flour essential — T65 forte or bread flour W260+ 🌾

recipe.yeastReassurance: Yes, that's intentional — less yeast, more time = deeper flavour. Trust the process. 🍕
recipe.precisionScale: Precision scale recommended — this is a very small amount of yeast.

bakeGuide.preheatNote: Start preheating while dough finishes its final proof — they should be ready at the same time.

stepsFridge.bold: Weigh equal parts flour and water
stepsFridge.note: see recipe for exact amounts — 100% hydration
very small amount — poolish ferments slowly

stepsFridge.bold: Stir vigorously until no dry flour remains

stepsFridge.note: press it to the surface to prevent skin
poolish ferments cold — slow and controlled

stepsRT.bold: Weigh equal parts flour and water
stepsRT.note: see recipe for exact amounts — 100% hydration
very small amount — poolish ferments slowly

stepsRT.bold: Stir vigorously until no dry flour remains

stepsRT.note: press it to the surface to prevent skin
poolish ferments at room temp for this schedule

readyWhen: Surface is domed and bubbly — looks like a lava lamp slowly bubbling
Slightly pulling away from the sides of the container
Just starting to flatten (use it now — don't wait for full collapse)
pitfalls: Over-fermented: strong alcohol smell, flat surface, watery — discard and start again
Fully collapsed poolish loses strength — check before the centre drops
Using warm water — use room temp or cold water for slow controlled fermentation

steps.bold: Weigh flour and water
steps.note: see recipe — biga is ~50-60% hydration, stiffer than poolish

steps.bold: Add a tiny amount of IDY
steps.note: 0.1-0.2% of flour — biga ferments very slowly

steps.bold: Mix roughly until just combined
steps.note: biga is intentionally shaggy — do not over-mix

steps.bold: Cover loosely
steps.note: biga needs some air exchange unlike poolish

steps.bold: Place in fridge
steps.note: biga always ferments cold — never at room temp

readyWhen: Surface has small cracks and bubbles — smells yeasty and slightly alcoholic
Slightly pulling away from the sides of the container
pitfalls: Over-fermented: strong alcohol smell, flat surface, watery — discard and start again
Biga that smells like acetone is over-fermented and unusable
Using warm water — always use cold water for biga

steps.bold: Discard all but 50g of starter
steps.note: fresh ratio ferments faster and more predictably

steps.bold: Add 50g flour + 50g water (1:1:1)
steps.note: adjust ratio for your target peak time

steps.bold: Stir vigorously until smooth
steps.note: no lumps

steps.bold: Mark the jar with a rubber band
steps.note: tracks rise visually

steps.bold: Leave uncovered or lightly covered
steps.note: starter needs air

readyWhen: Domed top with visible bubbles on surface and sides
Float test: drop a small piece in water — if it floats, it's ready
Smells tangy and yeasty — not alcoholic
pitfalls: Using starter past peak — it will have less strength and your dough will rise slowly
Hooch (dark liquid on top) means starter was hungry — pour it off, feed, wait for full rise before using

waterTempBullets: Use the water temperature from your recipe — Baker Hub calculated this accounting for your kitchen and mixer
Check FDT with a thermometer right after mixing — dough should feel cool to the touch
mix.watchForSmooth: Smooth, elastic ball — slightly tacky but not sticky
mix.watchForPumpkin: Pumpkin shape: dough wraps around the breaker bar, pulls cleanly from bowl walls
watchForAll: Windowpane test: stretch a small piece until light shows through without tearing
Skin looks smooth and slightly shiny
pitfalls: Adding salt and yeast at the same time — salt kills yeast on contact, always add separately
Over-kneading by hand is nearly impossible — but over-mixing in a stand mixer is not, stop when windowpane passes
Adding oil before gluten develops — oil coats proteins and blocks gluten formation

stepsBase.bold: Transfer dough to a lightly oiled container with a lid
stepsBase.note: clear container lets you monitor rise

stepsBase.bold: Mark the level with a rubber band

set1.note: wet hand, grab one side, stretch up then fold over centre. Repeat 4 sides

set2.note: dough should feel more cohesive and resist tearing

set3.note: dough will feel smooth and strong

set4.note: then leave undisturbed until bulk is done

setShort.bold: After 15 min: one set of stretch & folds
setShort.note: then cover and rest — short bulk goes straight to cold

setVeryShort.bold: Cover immediately and place in fridge
setVeryShort.note: bulk is very short — dough goes cold right away

watchFor: Dough has grown 50-75% (not doubled — pizza dough bulk is shorter than bread)
Slightly domed, bubbly surface
Jiggles like jelly when you shake the container
Feels airy and lighter than when you started
pitfallsBase: Over-bulk: more than 75% rise means gluten has started to break down — dough will be slack and tear during shaping
Under-bulk: less than 30% rise — not enough gas development, dough will be dense
Skipping stretch & folds — they build gluten strength that makes shaping much easier

steps.bold: Cover container tightly with cling film
steps.note: press to the surface — dough skin is the enemy

steps.bold: Place on a middle shelf at the back of the fridge
steps.note: back of fridge is coldest and most consistent

steps.bold: No need to check the dough
steps.note: cold fermentation is slow and forgiving

watchFor: Dough will grow slowly — 20-30% rise during cold retard is normal
Surface may look slightly domed and have small bubbles — this is perfect
Cold dough is stiff straight from the fridge — this is correct, it makes divide & ball easier
pitfalls: Leaving uncovered: dough skin forms, tears during shaping and creates uneven balls
Rushing the cold phase: minimum cold time is important for flavour and gluten relaxation

steps.bold: Divide into equal pieces
steps.note: use a scale for even pieces

steps.bold: Flatten each piece gently with your hands
steps.note: aim for roughly oval, 1.5–2cm thick

steps.bold: Cut the leaf pattern with a sharp knife or dough cutter
steps.note: one central slash, 4–5 angled cuts each side

steps.bold: Open the cuts slightly with your fingers
steps.note: spread them apart so they do not close during baking

steps.bold: Transfer to a lined baking tray
steps.note: dust with flour — fougasse does not go in the fridge

steps.bold: Cover and leave at room temperature for final proof
steps.note: 20–40 min at room temperature

watchFor: Cuts stay open — if they close, reopen with your fingers
Even thickness — thick spots will be dense, thin spots will burn
Surface dusted with flour — prevents sticking to tray
pitfalls: Cuts closing back up — open them wider and dust with more flour
Over-handling: fougasse is a flatbread, not a structured loaf — gentle is better
Too thin at the edges — they will burn before the centre is cooked

steps.bold: Divide into equal pieces
steps.note: use a scale — equal pieces

steps.bold: Pre-shape: fold to a loose rectangle
steps.note: do not degas — just gently fold sides in

steps.bold: Cover and bench rest 20 min
steps.note: gluten relaxes, makes final shaping easier

steps.bold: Final shape: roll gently into a baguette
steps.note: use your fingertips, not your palm — length ~30–40cm

steps.bold: Place in a floured couche or baguette tray
steps.note: seam side up in couche, seam side down in tray

watchFor: Even cylinder, tapered ends — uniform diameter bakes evenly
Tight seam — open seam bursts uncontrollably in the oven
Dough springs back slowly when poked — ready to score and bake
pitfalls: Over-flouring: reduces surface tension, baguette spreads instead of holding shape
Pressing too hard: degasses the dough — work gently to keep the bubbles
Skipping the bench rest: gluten tears during final shaping

steps.bold: Divide into equal pieces
steps.note: use a scale for even loaves

steps.bold: Shape each piece into a log
steps.note: flatten slightly, roll from top to bottom, seal the seam

steps.bold: Place seam-side down in a greased loaf tin
steps.note: tin should be about two-thirds full

watchFor: Smooth top, no tears — tight skin is important for oven spring
Dough fills about 2/3 of the tin after shaping
Before baking: dough should dome just above the tin rim
pitfalls: Greasing the tin: use butter or spray oil — without it the loaf sticks
Over-proofing in the tin: dough should dome slightly, not overflow
Uneven shaping: log should be the same width as the tin

steps.bold: Divide into equal pieces
steps.note: use a scale — equal pieces

steps.bold: Pre-shape: fold to a rough round
steps.note: fold edges to centre, flip seam-side down, rest 15–20 min

steps.bold: Final shape: build surface tension
steps.note: cup hands around the dough, drag toward you on unfloured surface

steps.bold: Place in a floured banneton, seam side up
steps.note: dust generously with rice flour to prevent sticking

watchFor: Smooth, taut surface — no tears or wrinkles
Holds its round shape without spreading
If the dough tears easily, gluten is still warm — rest 5 min and try again
pitfalls: Flouring the bench: reduces friction, ball won't develop surface tension — use bare, slightly damp surface
Skipping the bench rest: gluten tears when it's too tight
Under-tensioning: a slack ball spreads flat and won't hold shape in the oven

steps.bold: Weigh dough and divide into equal pieces
steps.note: use a scale — eyeballing leads to uneven baking

steps.bold: Pre-shape each piece into a rough round
steps.note: fold edges to centre, flip seam-side down

steps.bold: Tuck and drag: cup your hand over the ball
steps.note: drag it toward you on an unfloured surface — creates tension

steps.bold: Place in individual dough boxes or bowls
steps.note: seam side down, well-spaced

watchFor: Smooth, taut skin with no tears or folds visible on top
Holds its round shape — doesn't immediately spread flat (if it does, gluten is weak)
pitfalls: Flouring the surface: reduces friction, ball won't get surface tension — use bare, slightly damp surface
Tearing the skin during shaping — pre-shape roughly first, rest 5 min, then final ball

coverRT.bold: Cover and leave at room temperature

steps.bold: Cover each dough box tightly
steps.note: balls must not dry out during cold retard

steps.bold: Stack in the fridge — back shelf
steps.note: temperature consistency is critical

watchFor: Balls will grow slightly in the fridge — 20-40% rise is normal
Cold balls are firm and easy to handle — this is exactly what you want
After 24h+ the balls will have relaxed gluten and will be extremely extensible when warm
pitfalls: Over-retarding: balls that have risen more than 100% in the fridge are over-fermented — bake immediately when removed
Cold balls that tear on stretching: need more warmup time — never stretch cold dough

removeFridge.note: keep covered — do not stretch yet, gluten is cold and tight

pokeTest.bold: Start poke test after warmup — every 15–20 min
pokeTest.note: do not go by time — go by feel

pokeResponses: Springs back immediately, feels tight — needs more time
Springs back slowly and partially — ready to bake
Does not spring back, feels slack — over-proofed, bake immediately
pitfalls: Stretching cold dough — wait for warmup, cold gluten tears
Going by time instead of feel — always use the poke test
Over-proofed balls collapse in the oven and lose oven spring

steps.bold: Place Dutch oven (with lid) inside your oven
steps.note: both pot and lid must be scorching hot

steps.bold: Set oven to max with fan
steps.note: full preheat — 45 min minimum

steps.bold: Do not open the oven during preheat
steps.note: every opening loses 20–30°C

pitfalls: Skipping the lid: steam is what creates the ear and glossy crust — lid on for first 20 min is non-negotiable
Cold Dutch oven: pot must be scorching hot or the bottom will not colour properly

steps.bold: Place baking stone or steel on middle rack
steps.note: stone needs 45–60 min to fully absorb heat

steps.bold: Place an empty metal tray on the rack below
steps.note: for steam — you will add ice cubes at load time

steps.bold: Set oven to max with fan
steps.note: as hot as your oven allows

steps.bold: Do not open the oven during preheat
steps.note: every opening loses 20–30°C

pitfalls: Cutting preheat short: stone needs 45–60 min of heat to absorb enough thermal mass
Forgetting the steam tray: without steam the crust sets before oven spring finishes

steps.bold: Set steam oven with 100% steam
steps.note: steam programme for first phase

steps.bold: Allow full preheat — 20–30 min
steps.note: cavity must be fully saturated with steam

pitfalls: Under-saturating the cavity: run full preheat with steam before loading
Switching off steam too early: keep steam on for the full first 20 min

steps.bold: Build fire and let it burn to embers
steps.note: aim for 280–320°C floor temperature

steps.bold: Push embers to one side — test floor temp with a hand
steps.note: 3 seconds before pulling away = ready

steps.bold: Let temperature stabilise before loading
steps.note: even heat is more important than peak heat

pitfalls: Loading on a hot floor: let it recover after clearing embers, or the bottom burns
Uneven heat: rotate the loaf halfway through for even crust colour

steps.bold: Set oven to max with fan
steps.note: as hot as your oven allows

steps.bold: Place an empty metal tray on the rack below
steps.note: for steam — add ice cubes or boiling water at load time

steps.bold: Do not open during preheat
steps.note: every opening loses 20–30°C

pitfalls: Forgetting steam: without it the crust sets too early — no ear, pale and tough
Too low a temperature: standard ovens struggle — always use max

steps.bold: Light the fire and build to a high flame
steps.note: target 450–500°C floor temperature

steps.bold: Push fire to back or side — let floor recover
steps.note: floor temp drops when loading — give it time

steps.bold: Check floor with infrared thermometer
steps.note: never guess — launch only when floor is at temp

steps.bold: Allow full 45 min — flame active throughout
steps.note: stone must be saturated, not just surface-hot

pitfalls: Launching on a cold floor: always check with a thermometer — looks hot does not mean it is hot
Flame too high at launch: push back the fire before loading or the top burns before the base is cooked
Forgetting to rotate: wood-fired ovens have a hot side — turn the pizza every 20–30 seconds

steps.bold: Set both top and bottom elements to maximum
steps.note: most models reach temp in 15–20 min

steps.bold: Do not open lid during preheat
steps.note: electric ovens lose heat fast — keep closed until ready

steps.bold: Check stone temp with infrared thermometer
steps.note: stone should read 380°C+ before launching

pitfalls: Opening lid mid-bake: electric ovens have a small cavity — every opening drops temp significantly
Under-preheating: stone needs 20–25 min — do not trust the oven's ready indicator alone

steps.bold: Place stone or steel on the top rack
steps.note: close to the top element — top heat drives leoparding

steps.bold: Set oven to maximum temperature with fan
steps.note: full 60 min preheat

steps.bold: Switch to grill/broil for the last 10 min
steps.note: supercharges the top element for better char

steps.bold: Do not open during preheat
steps.note: every opening loses 20–30°C

pitfalls: Stone too low in the oven: top heat is what drives leoparding — use the top rack
Skipping the pre-grill phase: 10 min on grill/broil before launch supercharges the top element
Cutting preheat short: steel needs 45–60 min to absorb enough heat

steps.bold: Set oven to maximum temperature
steps.note: as hot as it goes

steps.bold: Preheat pizza tray or heavy baking sheet
steps.note: place directly on middle rack — must be hot

steps.bold: Allow 30 min minimum
steps.note: thin trays heat fast but lose heat fast too — longer is better

pitfalls: Thin baking tray: loses heat instantly at load — use the heaviest tray you have
Not preheating the tray: cold tray = white soggy bottom
Over-topping: heavy toppings prevent the base from cooking through

steps.bold: Score the dough — single slash or cross
steps.note: sharp lame or razor at 30–45° angle — confident single motion

steps.bold: Lower dough into Dutch oven using parchment
steps.note: work quickly — every second counts

steps.bold: Bake covered at 240°C — 20 min
steps.note: steam trapped inside creates the ear and oven spring

steps.bold: Remove lid — bake 20–25 min more
steps.note: crust browns and crisps — internal temp 95–98°C

steps.bold: Cool on a wire rack — minimum 30 min
steps.note: crumb is still cooking from residual heat — cutting hot makes it gummy

watchFor: Oven spring: bread grows noticeably in first 10 min — this is the yeast's last burst
Ear: the scoring line opens up and creates a defined ridge — sign of good fermentation and steam
Colour: deep mahogany brown — pale bread is under-baked regardless of time
Hollow sound: tap the bottom — should sound hollow when fully baked
pitfalls: Scoring too shallow: less than 0.5cm — won't open properly, creates side blowouts
Removing lid too early: steam needs the full 20 min to form the ear — patience
Cutting bread hot: steam still inside — crumb will be gummy and dense

steps.bold: Score the dough
steps.note: sharp lame, 30–45° angle, confident motion

steps.bold: Add ice cubes to steam tray immediately
steps.note: do this just before or just after loading — not before

steps.bold: Load onto hot stone — close oven fast
steps.note: confident single motion with a peel or parchment

steps.bold: Bake 20 min with steam
steps.note: do not open the door — steam must stay in

steps.bold: Remove steam tray — bake 20–25 min more
steps.note: crust browns and crisps — internal temp 95–98°C

steps.bold: Cool on a wire rack — minimum 30 min
steps.note: cutting hot makes the crumb gummy

watchFor: Oven spring: bread grows noticeably in first 10 min
Ear: scoring line opens into a defined ridge
Colour: deep mahogany brown
Hollow sound when tapped on the bottom
pitfalls: Scoring too shallow: less than 0.5cm
Opening the oven in the first 20 min: steam escapes, crust sets too early, no ear
Cutting bread hot: steam still inside — crumb will be gummy and dense

steps.bold: Score the dough
steps.note: sharp lame, 30–45° angle

steps.bold: Load into steam oven — 240°C, 100% steam
steps.note: bake 20 min — steam does the work of a Dutch oven

steps.bold: Switch to dry heat — 220°C
steps.note: bake 20–25 min more until deep brown and hollow-sounding

steps.bold: Cool on a wire rack — minimum 30 min
steps.note: cutting hot makes the crumb gummy

pitfalls: Not managing steam in the first phase: ear and oven spring both depend on it
Cutting bread hot: steam still inside — crumb will be gummy and dense

steps.bold: Score the dough
steps.note: sharp lame, 30–45° angle

steps.bold: Load using a long-handled peel
steps.note: confident single forward motion — slide, do not push

steps.bold: Close oven door or damper for first 15 min
steps.note: retain steam from the dough — creates ear

steps.bold: Rotate loaf halfway — bake until deep brown
steps.note: total 40–50 min — hollow sound at base = done

steps.bold: Cool on a wire rack — minimum 30 min
steps.note: cutting hot makes the crumb gummy

pitfalls: Scoring too shallow: less than 0.5cm
Cold floor at launch: always check with a thermometer
Not rotating: wood-fired ovens have a hot side — rotate halfway for even crust colour

steps.bold: Score the dough
steps.note: sharp lame or razor, 30–45° angle

steps.bold: Add boiling water or ice to steam tray, load quickly
steps.note: close oven door immediately to keep steam in

steps.bold: Bake 20 min — do not open door
steps.note: steam keeps crust extensible for oven spring

steps.bold: Remove steam tray — bake 20–25 min more
steps.note: internal temp 95–98°C — deep brown and hollow-sounding

steps.bold: Cool on a wire rack — minimum 30 min
steps.note: cutting hot makes the crumb gummy

pitfalls: Scoring too shallow: less than 0.5cm
Opening the oven in the first 20 min: steam escapes, crust sets too early
Cutting bread hot: steam still inside — crumb will be gummy and dense

steps.bold: Stretch dough to target size
steps.note: no rolling pin — knuckles and gravity only

steps.bold: Top quickly — sauce, cheese, minimal toppings
steps.note: work fast — wet toppings stick the base to the peel

steps.bold: Check floor temp one last time
steps.note: launch only above 400°C floor

steps.bold: Launch with a confident forward motion
steps.note: hesitation causes sticking — one smooth push

steps.bold: Rotate every 20–30 sec with a turning peel
steps.note: wood fire has a hot side — constant rotation is key

steps.bold: Total bake: 60–90 sec
steps.note: leoparding on cornicione + slight char on base = done

watchFor: Leoparding: dark spots on the cornicione — sign of proper fermentation and high heat
Puffing: dough bubbles up in the centre — normal and desirable for Neapolitan
Base colour: golden with some dark spots — check with turning peel
Cheese: melted and slightly golden at edges — not bubbling brown
pitfalls: Cold floor at launch: always check with a thermometer — looks hot does not mean it is hot
Flame too close: push fire back before launching or the top burns before the base is done
Not rotating: wood-fired ovens have a hot side — rotate every 20–30 sec or expect uneven char

steps.bold: Stretch dough to target size
steps.note: no rolling pin — electric ovens are forgiving but thin bases still bake best

steps.bold: Top and launch onto hot stone
steps.note: flour or fine semolina on peel — work quickly

steps.bold: Close lid immediately
steps.note: electric ovens have small cavities — heat escapes fast

steps.bold: Rotate halfway for even colour
steps.note: electric elements can have hot spots near the edges

watchFor: Cornicione: starts pale, then yellows, then browns — pull when it starts showing spots
Base: check by lifting edge — should be golden brown with some colour
pitfalls: Opening lid mid-bake: small cavity loses heat very quickly
Under-preheating: stone needs 20–25 min — do not trust the oven's ready indicator alone
Thick bases: electric pizza ovens suit thin Neapolitan and NY — thicker styles may under-bake the base

steps.bold: Stretch dough on a floured peel
steps.note: semolina or flour — not too much or the base will be dusty

steps.bold: Top quickly and launch onto hot stone
steps.note: confident single forward motion — hesitation causes sticking

steps.bold: Switch to grill/broil immediately after launch
steps.note: top heat is what drives leoparding in a home oven

steps.bold: Bake 5–7 min
steps.note: watch the cheese and cornicione — grill moves fast

steps.bold: Check base with a palette knife or spatula
steps.note: should be golden with some colour, not white

watchFor: Top colour from the grill: cornicione should show dark spots within 5–7 min
Base: lift with a spatula — golden and firm, not pale or soft
Cheese: bubbling and golden — if cheese is done but cornicione is pale, flash it under grill
pitfalls: Skipping the grill/broil phase: top heat is what drives leoparding — without it the pizza looks pale
Stone too low: must be on the top rack to get close to the top element
Cutting preheat short: steel needs 45–60 min to absorb enough thermal mass

steps.bold: Stretch dough on a floured surface
steps.note: thicker styles work best here — Detroit, pan, Roman

steps.bold: Top generously — standard ovens suit loaded styles
steps.note: toppings help retain moisture and colour evenly

steps.bold: Place on preheated tray — bake at max temperature
steps.note: 8–15 min depending on thickness

steps.bold: Rotate halfway through
steps.note: all home ovens have hot spots near the element

steps.bold: Check base: lift edge with a spatula
steps.note: base should be golden and set, not white or soft

watchFor: Base: lift edge with a spatula — should be golden and set
Cheese: melted and beginning to colour at edges
Crust: golden and puffed at the rim — pale crust means more time or higher heat needed
pitfalls: Cold tray: must be preheated or the base will be pale and soft
Over-topping: too many wet toppings prevent the base from cooking through
Low temperature: always use the highest temperature your oven reaches

steps.bold: Stretch or shape dough
steps.note: use your preferred method

steps.bold: Top and bake at maximum temperature
steps.note: time depends on oven and thickness

steps.bold: Rotate halfway for even bake
steps.note: all ovens have hot spots

prefStatus.inFridge: 🧊 In fridge — ready at Start Dough
prefStatus.notYetPeak: 🟡 Not yet at peak — still developing
